abstract:
Unraveling the atomic configuration of a catalyst under operational conditions is essential to identifying the fundamental drivers of its performance. Because a catalyst’s structure rarely remains static when transitioning from synthesis to an active reaction environment, in situ characterization is the gold standard for such studies. In this work, we employ in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to investigate $\text{Pt}_3\text{Ni}$ hollow nanoframes, a class of materials recognized as world-class electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). 

To probe the surface properties of these nanoframes, we utilized electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition ($\text{H}_{\text{upd}}$) and carbon monoxide electrooxidation, revealing that structural variations in the nanoframes lead to distinct binding strengths with adsorbate molecules. It is established that the formation of a "Pt-skin" on Pt-Ni alloys significantly boosts ORR activity by optimizing (weakening) these surface binding energies. Our comparative ex situ and in situ XAS analysis demonstrates that nanoframes with stronger adsorbate binding possess a rougher Pt surface, a result of insufficient Pt segregation and subsequent Ni dissolution. In contrast, nanoframes exhibiting exceptionally high ORR activity show a more pronounced segregation of Pt over Ni-rich subsurface layers, facilitating the formation of a high-quality Pt-skin. This study highlights that the superior catalytic performance of $\text{Pt}_3\text{Ni}$ nanoframes is directly contingent upon the successful atomic-level engineering of the Pt-skin surface structure.
